section.title: The Exiles Who Returned
section.sectionRationale: After the decree comes the register. Chapter two names the families, towns, priests, Levites, servants, and even the livestock of those who came home, closing with their arrival and their freewill giving. It reads like a ledger, but it is a roll of honor: God records His remnant by name and settles them back in the land to rebuild His house.
section.thesis: God knows and numbers His returning remnant person by person, guarding the purity of His worship and settling His people in their towns, so that the long list of names becomes a testimony that not one of His own is forgotten.
section.frameNote: A single register in movements: the leaders and men of Israel by family and town, verses 1 to 35; the priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, temple servants, and Solomon's servants, verses 36 to 58; those who could not prove their lineage, verses 59 to 63; the totals and the arrival with freewill offerings, verses 64 to 70.

units.label: The people of the province by family and town

verses.text: Now these are the people of the {{medinah|province}} who came up from the captivity of the exiles carried away to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar its king. They returned to Jerusalem and Judah, each to his own town,
accompanied by Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, and Baanah. This is the count of the men of Israel:
the descendants of Parosh, 2,172;
the descendants of Shephatiah, 372;
the descendants of Arah, 775;
the descendants of Pahath-moab (through the line of Jeshua and Joab), 2,812;
the descendants of Elam, 1,254;
the descendants of Zattu, 945;
the descendants of Zaccai, 760;
the descendants of Bani, 642;
the descendants of Bebai, 623;
the descendants of Azgad, 1,222;
the descendants of Adonikam, 666;
the descendants of Bigvai, 2,056;
the descendants of Adin, 454;
the descendants of Ater (through Hezekiah), 98;
the descendants of Bezai, 323;
the descendants of Jorah, 112;
the descendants of Hashum, 223;
the descendants of Gibbar, 95;
the men of Bethlehem, 123;
the men of Netophah, 56;
the men of Anathoth, 128;
the descendants of Azmaveth, 42;
the men of Kiriath-jearim, Chephirah, and Beeroth, 743;
the men of Ramah and Geba, 621;
the men of Michmash, 122;
the men of Bethel and Ai, 223;
the descendants of Nebo, 52;
the descendants of Magbish, 156;
the descendants of the other Elam, 1,254;
the descendants of Harim, 320;
the men of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, 725;
the men of Jericho, 345;
and the descendants of Senaah, 3,630.

units.label: The servants of the house of God
units.note: priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and temple servants

verses.text: The priests: The descendants of Jedaiah (through the house of Jeshua), 973;
the descendants of Immer, 1,052;
the descendants of Pashhur, 1,247;
and the descendants of Harim, 1,017.
The Levites: the descendants of Jeshua and Kadmiel (through the line of Hodaviah), 74.
The singers: the descendants of Asaph, 128.
The gatekeepers: the descendants of Shallum, the descendants of Ater, the descendants of Talmon, the descendants of Akkub, the descendants of Hatita, and the descendants of Shobai, 139 in all.
The temple servants: the descendants of Ziha, the descendants of Hasupha, the descendants of Tabbaoth,
the descendants of Keros, the descendants of Siaha, the descendants of Padon,
the descendants of Lebanah, the descendants of Hagabah, the descendants of Akkub,
the descendants of Hagab, the descendants of Shalmai, the descendants of Hanan,
the descendants of Giddel, the descendants of Gahar, the descendants of Reaiah,
the descendants of Rezin, the descendants of Nekoda, the descendants of Gazzam,
the descendants of Uzza, the descendants of Paseah, the descendants of Besai,
the descendants of Asnah, the descendants of Meunim, the descendants of Nephusim,
the descendants of Bakbuk, the descendants of Hakupha, the descendants of Harhur,
the descendants of Bazluth, the descendants of Mehida, the descendants of Harsha,
the descendants of Barkos, the descendants of Sisera, the descendants of Temah,
the descendants of Neziah, and the descendants of Hatipha.
The descendants of the servants of Solomon: the descendants of Sotai, the descendants of Hassophereth, the descendants of Peruda,
the descendants of Jaala, the descendants of Darkon, the descendants of Giddel,
the descendants of Shephatiah, the descendants of Hattil, the descendants of Pochereth-hazzebaim, and the descendants of Ami.
The temple servants and descendants of the servants of Solomon numbered 392 in all.

units.label: Those who could not prove their lineage
units.note: excluded until a priest could consult the Urim and Thummim

verses.text: The following came up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addan, and Immer, but they could not prove that their families were descended from Israel:
the descendants of Delaiah, the descendants of Tobiah, and the descendants of Nekoda, 652 in all.

And from among the priests: the descendants of Hobaiah, the descendants of Hakkoz, and the descendants of Barzillai (who had married a daughter of Barzillai the Gileadite and was called by their name).
These men searched for their {{yachas|family records}}, but they could not find them and so were excluded from the priesthood as unclean.
The governor ordered them not to eat the most holy things until there was a priest to consult the {{urim|Urim and Thummim}}.

units.label: The totals and the arrival
units.note: settled in their towns, giving freely

verses.text: The whole assembly numbered 42,360,
in addition to their 7,337 menservants and maidservants, as well as their 200 male and female singers.
They had 736 horses, 245 mules,
435 camels, and 6,720 donkeys.
When they arrived at the house of the LORD in Jerusalem, some of the heads of the families gave {{nedabah|freewill offerings}} to rebuild the house of God on its original site.
According to their ability, they gave to the treasury for this work 61,000 darics of gold, 5,000 minas of silver, and 100 priestly garments.
So the priests, the Levites, the singers, the gatekeepers, and the temple servants, along with some of the people, settled in their own towns; and the rest of the Israelites settled in their towns.

medinah.gloss: A province or administrative district. Judah is now no longer a kingdom but a small province within the Persian empire. The word quietly marks how far the nation had fallen, and yet it is precisely into this humble province that God gathers His remnant to rebuild.

yachas.gloss: To register by genealogy, to be enrolled in a family record. The whole chapter is a yachas, an enrollment of the remnant. For the priests especially, a verifiable record mattered: worship had to be led by those God had truly set apart, and identity in the covenant people was traced through recorded lineage.

urim.gloss: The Urim and Thummim, the sacred means by which the high priest discerned God's decision, which the BSB footnote renders Lights and Perfections. Its absence here shows a community waiting on God for clear guidance, and it points ahead to Christ, in whom God's will and light are fully revealed.

nedabah.gloss: A freewill offering, given gladly and uncoerced. The register ends not with obligation but with generosity: having been brought home by grace, the family heads give willingly toward the rebuilding of God's house.

basic.context: This chapter is the official register of everyone who came home in the first return, listed by family, by town, and by role in worship. It counts leaders, ordinary Israelites, priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, temple servants, and even the animals. It flags some who could not prove they belonged, and it ends with the people arriving in Jerusalem and giving freely to rebuild the temple. The question under all the names is: does God really know and keep each of His people? Yes, He does.
basic.hermeneutics: A long list of names and numbers can feel skippable, but it is Scripture on purpose. It is a historical record, not a set of commands. The point is not to imitate the census but to see what it reveals: God preserves a named remnant, He cares about the purity of those who lead His worship, and He settles His people in their inheritance. Read it as a roll of honor that says God forgets no one.
basic.originalLanguages: Judah is now only a province, a medinah, a shrunken shadow of a kingdom. The chapter is a yachas, a genealogical enrollment, and it matters most for the priests. Where records fail, the people wait for the Urim and Thummim, God's means of guidance. And it all closes with the nedabah, the glad freewill offering.
basic.typology: The register of the returning people points to the book of life, in which God writes the names of His own. The concern that priests prove their descent points to Christ, our great High Priest, whose right to serve is certain and whose credentials open the way for us.
basic.christ: God keeps a careful record of His people, and no name is lost. That care finds its fullness in Jesus, who says He knows His sheep by name and that their names are written in heaven. The strict guarding of the priesthood, letting only those with verified descent serve, highlights our need for a priest whose qualification is beyond question. Jesus is that priest, and through Him even outsiders are brought near.
basic.god: God is faithful to individuals, not just to the nation as a whole. He numbers the survivors, watches over the holiness of His worship, and provides for those unsure of their standing by pointing them to His own guidance. He is a God of order, mercy, and detail.
basic.crossReferences: Nehemiah 7 records almost the same list. Philippians 4:3 and Revelation 21:27 speak of names written in the book of life. And Hebrews 7 shows Christ as the High Priest whose priesthood needs no genealogy from Levi.
basic.oneStory: The remnant God promised to preserve is here, counted and named. Every name is a small proof that God keeps His people through exile and brings them home, and it points forward to the day when the full roll of the redeemed, written in the Lamb's book of life, is gathered home in Christ.

context.literary: The chapter is a formal register, structured by category: lay families listed by ancestor, then by town, followed by the temple personnel from priests down to servants, then a section on those of doubtful descent, and finally the grand totals and the arrival. Nearly identical to Nehemiah 7, it functions as an official archive placed here to ground the return in verifiable reality.
context.historical: Persian provinces kept careful population and tax records, and returning communities needed to establish their claims to land and temple office. Genealogical proof was essential for priestly service under the Law. The mention of horses, mules, camels, and donkeys, and of gold darics and silver minas, reflects a real caravan making a real journey with real resources.
context.canonical: This list secures the continuity between pre-exilic Israel and the restored community: the same families, the same priestly houses, the same God. It stands in the Bible's wider concern with names and registers, from the tribal censuses of Numbers to the Lamb's book of life in Revelation, all testifying that God's people are known and numbered by Him.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To document that the returning community was the true, continuous remnant of Israel, known by name and family, with a rightly ordered priesthood, so that the rebuilding of the temple rested on a people God had preserved and gathered by His grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific census and its rules for priestly service; it does not prescribe genealogical record-keeping for the church, whose priesthood is now defined by union with Christ. What endures is the truth that God knows His people individually and cares deeply about the integrity of those who lead His worship.

debates.question: Why does Scripture devote a whole chapter to names and numbers?

views.label: Theological witness to the remnant
views.case: The list proves God kept His promise to preserve a remnant and gives covenant continuity a concrete, named form; every entry declares that God forgets no one and that the restoration is real, not merely hoped for.

views.label: Practical archive for land and office
views.case: The community needed verifiable records to reclaim ancestral towns and to establish who could serve as priest or Levite; the census is first an administrative document securing rights and duties in the restored province.

debates.bearing: Both are true and reinforce each other: the practical record becomes a theological testimony, showing a God who works through ordinary registers to keep and settle His named people.

debates.question: Was it right to exclude the priests who could not prove their descent?

views.label: Necessary guarding of holy worship
views.case: The Law tied priestly service to verified Aaronic descent; admitting unproven priests would have compromised the holiness of the altar, so the governor's caution honored God's clear command about who may draw near.

views.label: Pastoral restraint, not condemnation
views.case: They were not cast out but told to wait until a priest could consult the Urim and Thummim; the ruling protects worship while leaving the door open for God Himself to clarify their standing, a measured, hopeful decision.

debates.bearing: Either way, the episode underscores how seriously God takes the qualification of those who mediate His worship, deepening our gratitude for Christ, the High Priest whose right to serve is beyond dispute.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens by calling Judah a medinah, a province: the kingdom is now a district of a foreign empire, and yet God gathers His people there. The list itself is a yachas, a genealogical enrollment, and the word does its heaviest work with the priests, for whom a lost record means exclusion from the altar until God gives clarity. That clarity was to come through the urim wetummim, the Urim and Thummim, the priestly means of discerning God's will, here longed for and absent. The register ends on a bright note with the nedabah, the freewill offering: a people brought home by sheer grace now give gladly toward the house of the LORD. The vocabulary moves from humbled province, to careful record, to waiting for God's guidance, to glad generosity.

typology.type: The register of the named remnant
typology.fulfillment: The Lamb's book of life in which God writes the names of the redeemed, Philippians 4:3 and Revelation 21:27.

typology.type: The requirement of verified priestly descent
typology.fulfillment: Christ our High Priest whose qualification is certain and eternal, established not by Levitical genealogy but by the power of an indestructible life, Hebrews 7:14-16.

typology.type: Waiting for the Urim and Thummim for guidance
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the light of the world, in whom the will of God is fully and finally revealed, John 8:12 and Hebrews 1:1-2.

study.christ: A chapter of names quietly proclaims a God who loses no one, and that is the heart of the gospel. Jesus says He knows His sheep by name and rejoices that His people's names are written in heaven. The careful vetting of the priesthood, admitting only those whose descent could be proven, exposes our deepest need: a priest whose right to serve is certain, so that our approach to God rests on His worthiness and not our own uncertain record. Hebrews shows that Jesus is exactly that priest, qualified not by an ancestral list but by an indestructible life, and through Him even the doubtful and the outsider are welcomed to the most holy things.
study.god: God's faithfulness reaches down to the level of the individual. He does not merely restore a nation in the abstract; He numbers survivors family by family and town by town, proving that His covenant love is personal and precise. He is jealous for the holiness of His worship, taking seriously who may lead His people before Him. And He is generous in return, so that His restored people respond with glad, freewill giving. The God of this ledger is a God of order, holiness, mercy, and detailed, personal care.

crossReferences.note: The nearly identical register, reused when Nehemiah repopulated Jerusalem, confirming the record's importance.
Christ's priesthood, unlike the Levitical order tied to descent, rests on the power of an indestructible life.
Only those written in the Lamb's book of life enter the city, the ultimate register of God's people.
Rejoice that your names are written in heaven, the personal knowledge of God that the census foreshadows.

study.oneStory: The remnant God pledged to keep is here, enrolled by name, settled in their towns, and giving freely toward His house. What looks like a dry ledger is in truth a monument to grace: God brought these exact people home. It anticipates the final and greater register, the Lamb's book of life, in which the names of all Christ's redeemed are written, a people He will gather home to Himself and never lose.

quiz.q: What is the main point of this long list of names and numbers?

options.text: God preserved and knows His remnant by name, settling them back in the land.
options.note: The register is a testimony that God forgets none of His people.

options.text: It shows how wealthy the returning exiles had become.
options.note: There are resources listed, but the focus is the named, preserved remnant, not wealth.

options.text: It is only an administrative footnote with no spiritual value.
options.note: It is a practical record, but it carries real theological weight about God knowing His people.

options.text: It proves the exiles had abandoned their heritage.
options.note: The opposite: the list establishes their unbroken covenant identity.

quiz.q: Why were some priests excluded from serving?

options.text: They could not find their family records to prove their priestly descent.
options.note: Verses 61-62 explain they were excluded as unclean until their lineage could be established.

options.text: They had committed a public sin.
options.note: No sin is named; the issue was specifically unproven descent.

options.text: They refused to give freewill offerings.
options.note: Giving is mentioned later and of the whole people, not as the reason for exclusion.

options.text: They were foreigners who had never claimed to be priests.
options.note: They claimed priestly descent but could not document it.

quiz.q: What were the excluded priests told to wait for?

options.text: A priest who could consult the Urim and Thummim to discern God's decision.
options.note: Verse 63 defers the matter to God's guidance through the Urim and Thummim.

options.text: A new king to rule over Judah.
options.note: The matter awaited priestly guidance from God, not a king.

options.text: Permission from the Persian emperor.
options.note: The decision rested on God's guidance, not imperial permission.

options.text: They were told to serve immediately without conditions.
options.note: They were specifically restrained until their standing was clarified.

quiz.q: How does the register point forward to Christ?

options.text: God's careful record of His named people anticipates the Lamb's book of life, and the guarded priesthood points to Christ our certain High Priest.
options.note: The chapter foreshadows both the book of life and Christ's sure priesthood.

options.text: It teaches that only those with the right ancestry can be saved.
options.note: Under the new covenant, standing rests on union with Christ, not genealogy.

options.text: It shows that God is only interested in nations, not persons.
options.note: The chapter stresses the opposite: God's personal knowledge of individuals.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The themes of names, the book of life, and priesthood run straight to Christ.

quiz.q: How did the family heads respond when they arrived in Jerusalem?

options.text: They gave freewill offerings, according to their ability, to rebuild the house of God.
options.note: Verses 68-69 record their glad, generous giving toward the temple.

options.text: They demanded the government fund the whole project.
options.note: Earlier Cyrus assisted, but here the people themselves give willingly.

options.text: They delayed all giving until the temple was finished.
options.note: They gave at the outset, to enable the rebuilding.

options.text: They kept their wealth for themselves.
options.note: The text highlights their generosity, not self-interest.

questions: God recorded each returning family by name. How does it steady you to know that God knows you personally and forgets none of His own?
Judah was now only a small province, a shadow of its former self, yet God was at work there. Where do you need to trust God to work in a season that feels diminished or unimpressive?
The community guarded who could lead its worship. Why does it matter that Christ, our High Priest, is fully and forever qualified to bring you to God?
Some could not prove they belonged and had to wait on God for clarity. How do you respond when your own standing or calling feels uncertain, and where do you look for assurance?
The people gave freely, according to their ability, because grace had brought them home. What would glad, proportionate generosity toward God's work look like for you this week?

translationNotes.note: The list of leaders differs slightly from the parallel in Nehemiah 7:7, where some names appear in variant forms; such differences are normal in ancient transmission of name lists and do not affect the sense.
Urim and Thummim renders a Hebrew phrase the BSB footnote gives as Lights and Perfections, the priestly means of seeking God's decision, here longed for but not yet available.
Darics of gold and minas of silver are Persian-era measures; the BSB footnotes give approximate modern weights, underscoring the substantial, sacrificial scale of the people's giving.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 2:1-70; all 70 verses included. Hyphens in compound place names (for example Pahath-moab, Kiriath-jearim, Tel-melah) are ordinary hyphens, not em or en dashes. No em or en dashes remain.
